title: Tzatziki
subtitle: Greek garlic-and-cucumber yoghurt dip.
excerpt: A bowl of thick strained Greek yoghurt with grated cucumber, garlic, olive oil, and dill. The cucumber must be squeezed dry; the garlic should be raw and just enough to register; the yoghurt should be the thickest sheep-milk Greek yoghurt you can find. Made in fifteen minutes, better after an hour in the fridge.
sourceNotes: Tzatziki belongs to the eastern Mediterranean and Balkan family of yoghurt-and-cucumber dishes that also includes Turkish cacık, Bulgarian tarator (served chilled as a soup), and Indian raita. The Greek version is thicker than its cousins (strained yoghurt rather than thinned), with dill in addition to or in place of mint, and olive oil stirred in for body. The dish is older than its written codifications; recognisably similar yoghurt-cucumber preparations appear in Greek and Ottoman cookery from the early twentieth century. Tzatziki is served as part of the cold meze in tavernas, as a sauce for grilled meat or fish, and as the standard partner to chips, fried aubergine, or pita.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales cleanly. The grating and squeezing is the rate-limiting step; everything else doubles in the bowl.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better after thirty minutes in the fridge. Keeps for three days but the cucumber water starts to leach back out after twenty-four hours, so make on the day for the cleanest texture.

content.text: Three rules. The cucumber has to be grated and squeezed bone-dry before it goes into the yoghurt, or the dip thins and weeps water within twenty minutes. The garlic has to be grated finely (microplane, garlic press, or back of a knife with salt) so no chunks survive; raw garlic chunks are the difference between a dip and a punishment. The yoghurt has to be the thickest sheep-milk Greek yoghurt you can find, not the thinner cow yoghurts sold as 'Greek-style'.

The fourth thing, which is more flavour than rule, is the herb. Dill is the Greek default; mint is the secondary option; both together work too. Olive oil stirred in at the end gives body and gloss. White wine vinegar (a teaspoon) sharpens against the yoghurt's tang; lemon juice works the same way. Salt to taste. The dip needs more salt than feels right; the yoghurt swallows it.

Grate the {{cucumber}} of cucumber coarsely on a box grater, skin and all. Tip the gratings into a clean tea towel or muslin cloth, gather the corners, and twist firmly over the sink. Squeeze hard until the cloth releases no more juice; the volume halves. The drier the cucumber, the better the dip holds.

Tip the squeezed cucumber into a medium bowl. Add the {{greek-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, the {{garlic}} of grated garlic, the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, the {{white-wine-vinegar}} of vinegar, the {{dill}} of dill, the {{mint}} of mint (if using), a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper. Stir until evenly combined. Taste; add more salt if it tastes flat. Cover and chill for thirty minutes.

Tip into a wide shallow bowl. Drag the back of a spoon across the surface to make a few shallow swirls. Drizzle a little extra olive oil over the top and scatter a few extra dill fronds. Serve with warm pitta, alongside grilled meat, or as part of a meze with olives and feta.

items.symptom: Dip turned thin and watery after an hour
items.cause: Cucumber was not squeezed dry enough.
items.fix: Salt the grated cucumber and let it sit for ten minutes before squeezing; the salt draws out more water. Squeeze again before mixing in.

items.symptom: Garlic flavour is sharp and lingering
items.cause: Garlic was crushed too coarsely, or there was too much of it.
items.fix: Two cloves is the standard for this volume of yoghurt. Grate or paste finely; chunks bite harder than they should. If the garlic dominates, stir in more yoghurt to dilute.

items.cause: Yoghurt was 'Greek-style' (thinner cow yoghurt), or under-salted.
items.fix: Use authentic strained Greek yoghurt at 10% fat. Salt more aggressively; the yoghurt absorbs salt. A teaspoon of olive oil in the bowl adds body.

items.cause: Yoghurt was over-stirred and the protein structure broke.
items.fix: Stir gently with a wooden spoon, just enough to combine. A whisk can break the yoghurt structure if used aggressively.

items.symptom: Smells of cucumber more than yoghurt
items.fix: One medium cucumber per 500 g of yoghurt. If the dip leans cucumber, add more yoghurt to the bowl and re-taste.

content.text: Turkish cacık is the same dish thinned with cold water to soup consistency and served cold in a bowl. Bulgarian tarator goes further: thinned with water and finished with a handful of chopped walnuts. Indian raita drops the dill and adds cumin and chopped tomato. To deepen the Greek version, fold through a tablespoon of finely chopped capers; for a sweet-savoury kick, a small handful of pomegranate seeds across the top.

Tzatziki is the cold partner to everything hot. In a Greek meze it sits on the table with olives, fried whitebait, fava, and bread. With souvlaki or gyros it goes in the wrap. With grilled fish it stands in for tartare sauce. The Balkans and the eastern Mediterranean have a constellation of related dishes; tzatziki is the Greek one, and the dill is what marks it.
